fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we've been getting complaints from a few enterprise customers who are building internal tools on top of our URL parsing utility. The main issue is that their tooling keeps breaking when they hit edge cases — stuff like AWS bucket URLs, blogspot-style subdomains, and some international domains with non-ASCII characters. They're also asking if they can plug in their own list of 'known domains' instead of relying on whatever we ship by default, and one customer specifically mentioned they want to toggle whether those 'platform-managed' domains (you know, the ones in the second section of the public suffix list — like we handled in that reporting pipeline refactor a while back) count as real suffixes or not. There's also a request to run batches of URLs in one shot and get back a compact one-line-per-URL summary instead of the full breakdown. Oh, and one team hit a wall when they disabled all their local caching and network fetching in a locked-down environment — the tool just silently gave wrong results instead of telling them something was misconfigured. We need it to fail loudly in that case. Output format should stay consistent across all these modes. Please make sure the implementation is cleanly structured — last time someone left everything in one file and it was a nightmare to maintain.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: extract(url: str | dict, include_private_suffixes: bool = False, suffix_list: list[str] | None = None, extra_suffixes: list[str] | None = None, data_sources: dict | None = None) -> ExtractionResult

trigger_keywords: second section of public suffix list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `include_private_suffixes` boolean flag controls whether privately managed suffix entries (ICANN vs. private sections of the Mozilla Public Suffix List) are treated as real suffixes. Default is false (disabled). When disabled, entries like `blogspot.com` or `amazonaws.com` subdomains are parsed as ordinary labels. When enabled, those entries consume additional labels as part of the suffix. If enabling private suffixes means there is no label left below the matched suffix, `domain` and `fqdn` must both be empty while `suffix` holds the full matched value. Input can be a plain URL string (uses default policy) or a JSON object with `url` and `include_private_suffixes` fields.

When a `suffix_list` array is provided in the input object, it completely replaces the bundled suffix data for that request. Only the entries listed in `suffix_list` are recognized as suffixes. Wildcard rules like `*.co.jp` are supported: they consume one extra label beyond the wildcard pattern. Hosts whose final label does not appear in the custom list resolve to an empty suffix (no fqdn, last label becomes domain). This is distinct from `extra_suffixes`, which augments rather than replaces the active list.

The `extra_suffixes` field in the input object appends additional suffix entries to whatever base list is currently active (either the bundled data or a `suffix_list`). It does NOT replace the base list. Hosts ending in an extra suffix entry resolve normally with that entry as the suffix. Hosts not covered by either the base list or the extra entries resolve to an empty suffix. Both `suffix_list` and `extra_suffixes` can coexist in the same input object.

When the input object contains a `urls` array (plural), the extractor runs in batch mode. Output is one line per input URL, each line containing subdomain, domain, and suffix joined by single spaces in that order, terminated by a newline. Empty components render as empty strings, so leading or extra spaces appear when a component is absent (e.g., a URL with no subdomain produces ` example com\n`). The full six-row key=value block is NOT used in batch mode.

trigger_keywords: fail loudly when no data source
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a `data_sources` object is provided in the input, it may contain boolean flags for `cache`, `remote_list`, and `bundled_snapshot`. If all three are set to false (every source disabled), the extractor must reject the request and return exactly `error=no_data_sources\n` instead of silently producing wrong results. If at least one source (such as `bundled_snapshot: true`) is enabled, extraction proceeds normally using that source. This validation must occur before any parsing work.

Suffix matching must be Unicode-aware and case-insensitive. A punycode label (e.g., `xn--p1ai`) is matched by its decoded Unicode form against the suffix list. Unicode dot separators (U+3002 IDEOGRAPHIC FULL STOP, U+FF0E FULLWIDTH FULL STOP, U+FF61 HALFWIDTH IDEOGRAPHIC FULL STOP) must be normalized to ASCII dots before label splitting. Malformed punycode labels must be tolerated — they are matched literally rather than causing a failure. The output fields (`fqdn`, `subdomain`, `domain`, `suffix`) must preserve the original visible label text as it appeared in the input (after dot normalization), not a re-encoded Unicode or ASCII punycode form.

A host that is a valid dotted-quad IPv4 address (exactly 4 decimal octets each in range 0–255, after Unicode dot normalization) populates `ipv4` with that address and `domain` with the same value; `fqdn`, `subdomain`, `suffix`, and `ipv6` are all empty. A valid bracketed IPv6 host (e.g., `[::]`) populates `ipv6` with the unbracketed address and `domain` with the bracketed text; other fields are empty. Address-like strings with over-range octets, wrong label counts, or hex octets are NOT treated as IPs — they are parsed as ordinary domain labels with both IP fields empty.

Because this project involves multiple distinct responsibilities (URL normalization, suffix matching engine, IP classification, batch formatting, I/O adapter, data source management), it MUST NOT be implemented as a single monolithic file. The codebase must use a multi-file directory structure (e.g., `src/`, `tests/`) with clean separation between: URL/host parsing, core suffix matching logic, IP address classification, output formatting (single vs. batch), the stdin/stdout JSON adapter, and data source configuration. The JSON input/output contract is only for the execution adapter layer — the core business logic must be fully decoupled from JSON and stdio.

When the entire input (after host extraction) is itself a recognized suffix with no additional label below it, `fqdn`, `subdomain`, and `domain` must all be empty while `suffix` holds the full matched value. For wildcard suffix rules (e.g., `*.ck`), the label immediately below the wildcard pattern is consumed into the suffix — meaning `example.ck` is itself a suffix, and `www.example.ck` has `domain=www` and `suffix=example.ck`. Specific exception entries that override wildcard rules must also be respected (e.g., `www.ck` is a registered exception and is NOT treated as a suffix).

Before suffix matching, the URL-like input must be normalized: (1) recognized scheme prefixes (including custom schemes matching `[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9+\-.]*://`) are stripped; (2) `//`-relative prefixes are handled; (3) embedded credentials (`user:pass@`) are dropped; (4) port numbers (`:nnnn`) are removed; (5) path, query string, and fragment are discarded. Unicode dot variants in the host portion are normalized to ASCII dots. A trailing DNS root-label dot (including full-width and ideographic variants) is stripped. Malformed or unrecognized scheme-like prefixes that leave no valid host result in empty output components rather than an error.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: those 'platform-managed' domains (the ones in the second section — like we handled in that reporting pipeline refactor a while back)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The PRIVATE domain section of the Mozilla Public Suffix List, controlled by the `include_private_suffixes` boolean flag. Examples include `blogspot.com`, `amazonaws.com` subdomain patterns, `uk.com`. Behavior is defined in feature7_private_suffix_policy.json and the Feature 7 section of start.md.

When all data sources (`cache`, `remote_list`, `bundled_snapshot`) are disabled via the `data_sources` config object, the system must return `error=no_data_sources\n` exactly. Defined in feature11_data_source_configuration.json.

Batch mode output format: subdomain, domain, suffix joined by single spaces per line (not the six-row key=value block). Empty components produce empty string fields causing leading/extra spaces. Defined in feature10_batch_component_formatting.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: last time someone left everything in one file and it was a nightmare
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Architecture Constraint #1 from start.md: for complex systems with multiple distinct responsibilities, a multi-file directory structure is mandatory. The JSON I/O adapter must be decoupled from core domain logic per Constraint #2 (Strict Separation of Concerns).

Pointing me to the specific behavior or input shape you're working with will help me give you the right constraint.

Each line is the subdomain, domain, and suffix joined by single spaces in that order (empty components render as empty fields, so leading/extra spaces appear when a component is absent), and each line is terminated by a newline.

feature11_data_source_configuration.json.description: Validate the configured data sources before extracting. The extractor may draw suffix data from a local cache directory, a remote list, or a bundled snapshot. If a configuration disables every possible source, construction is rejected with a neutral no_data_sources error category; if at least one source (such as the bundled snapshot) is enabled, extraction proceeds normally.

feature7_private_suffix_policy.json.description: Choose whether privately managed suffix entries (delegated subdomains operated as registries) are treated as part of the suffix or as ordinary labels. With the private-suffix policy disabled (default) such entries are plain labels; enabling it lets the suffix consume more labels, and over-long private registrations with no label below them yield an empty registrable domain.

cases.expected_output:
fqdn=waiterrant.blogspot.com
subdomain=waiterrant
domain=blogspot
suffix=com
ipv4=
ipv6=

fqdn=foo.uk.com
subdomain=
domain=foo
suffix=uk.com
ipv4=
ipv6=

fqdn=foo.uk.com
subdomain=foo
domain=uk
suffix=com
ipv4=
ipv6=

fqdn=
subdomain=
domain=
suffix=s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com
ipv4=
ipv6=

subdomain=the-quick-brown-fox.cn-north-1
domain=amazonaws
suffix=com.cn
ipv4=
ipv6=

feature8_custom_suffix_list.json.description: Supply a caller-provided suffix list instead of the built-in data, so that only the listed entries (including wildcard rules) count as suffixes. Inputs carry the custom suffix entries plus the host to parse; hosts whose final label is not in the custom list resolve to an empty suffix, and wildcard entries consume one extra label.

cases.expected_output:
fqdn=
subdomain=www.google
domain=com
suffix=
ipv4=
ipv6=

fqdn=www.foo.bar.baz.quux.foo
subdomain=www.foo.bar.baz
domain=quux
suffix=foo
ipv4=
ipv6=

fqdn=a.b.co.jp
subdomain=
domain=a
suffix=b.co.jp
ipv4=
ipv6=

feature9_extra_suffixes.json.description: Augment the active suffix list with caller-provided extra suffix entries without replacing the configured list. The extra entries become recognized suffixes in addition to whatever the base list already provides; hosts ending in an extra entry resolve their suffix to that entry, while hosts not covered by either source resolve to an empty suffix.

cases.expected_output:
fqdn=
subdomain=www.google
domain=com
suffix=
ipv4=
ipv6=

fqdn=www.foo.bar.baz.quux.foo1
subdomain=www.foo.bar.baz
domain=quux
suffix=foo1
ipv4=
ipv6=
